data.title: Lockheed Martin - Overbought After A Strong Run
data.description:
Introduction and Thesis

In this article I initiate coverage of Lockheed Martin (LMT). My thesis is that despite a wide competitive moat and number of robust business lines, the recent 33% increase in the stock price over a month and a half is too much too soon. The stock, currently trading at $579, is about 16% overvalued relative to my target price of $498. Although I believe the stock can achieve slightly higher near-term upside on the back of recent momentum, I rate it a sell at the current valuation.

Company Overview

Lockheed Martin (LMT) is the dominant company in the global aerospace and defense sector. The company is the largest prime contractor for the US Department of Defense, generating the vast majority of its revenues from the US government, though international sales are also expanding.

LMT is structured across four principal segments as follows (note that all the facts and figures in this section and the next section are sourced from the company's most recent 10K):

Aeronautics

This is Lockheed’s largest segment, supported by the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter program (accounting for ~26% of total sales). It also includes the F-16, C-130 Hercules, F-22 Raptor, and other highly classified development activities. This segment has a higher proportion of sales to international customers than the other segments (32% of Aeronautics net sales in 2024). Aeronautics was also the most profitable segment in 2024 with $2.5 billion in operating profit.

Rotary and Mission Systems

This segment encompasses rotary-wing aircraft like the Black Hawk helicopter, CH-53K Stallion (recent $10.9 billion contract for 99 helicopters), and Aegis naval combat systems. This segment has faced operational challenges recently on programs like the Canadian Maritime and Turkish Utility Helicopter programs. Lockheed cut guidance for 2025 sales in this category by $500 million.

Missiles and Fire Control

The MFC segment specializes in precision munitions and missile defense, including products like PAC-3 (over $2 billion in sales through Q3 2025), THAAD, HIMARS, and JASSM (recent $9.5 billion award). This segment presents a significant growth opportunity for the company, in particular through the Golden Dome program.

Space

LMT’s Space segment also presents significant opportunities for growth. The company recently acquired Terran Orbital to bolster its capabilities in space. The segment also includes the Trident II D5 missile, hypersonic systems, and satellite operations. The Space segment reached a $38 billion backlog in Q3 2025, in part due to Next Gen Interceptor program awards. Space Sales increased 9% YoY, with operating profit up 22% YoY.

Key Revenue Drivers

US Government Funding: The “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” passed in July 2025 provides approximately $156 billion in mandatory defense funding through FY 2029 in addition to regular appropriations. This is an unusual outcome, as most defense spending is set through annual authorization and appropriations processes, and it creates additional budget authority that could support Pentagon programs relevant to Lockheed’s backlog. However, this funding authority still requires appropriation and program execution before it translates into revenue, so it is supportive of the spending environment rather than a guarantee of future sales.

Geopolitical Demand: Conflicts in Ukraine, the Middle East, and Pacific drive secular demand for munitions and missile defense. The Ukraine War in particular has led to a depletion in the US munitions stockpile, including some of LMT's products like hellfire missiles and GMLRS systems. History suggests that conflict is persistent, meaning that LMT's products are likely to have enduring demand.

Record Backlog: A backlog of $179 billion as of Q3 2025 provides sustainable revenue visibility, with about 60% of the backlog expected to be converted to sales within 24 months.

Golden Dome Project: Lockheed is expected to be a major supplier to the planned Golden Dome Defense project. The company recently completed environmental testing of its next-gen GEO-based missile warning satellite, which is on track for delivery to Space Force. The project creates strong growth opportunities for the company's Space Systems segment in particular.

I created the Sankey chart below to help readers visualize how Lockheed makes money. The chart's data is sourced from the company's most recent 10K, meaning that it shows a breakdown for 2024's sales. When the company releases its full year 2025 results on January 29th, 2026, I'll update the chart in a new article and compare the differences between 2025 and 2024.

Key Expense Drivers

Fixed-Price Contract Risks: The most immediate challenge to profitability is exposure to fixed-price development contracts. "Reach-forward losses", where costs exceed transaction prices, totaled ~$2 billion in 2024.

Supply Chain & Labor Fragility: Component shortages, in particular semiconductors, and labor inflation have eroded profitability on fixed-price contracts.

Pension costs: Unfunded pension liabilities of $4.8 billion require substantial cash contributions ($990 million in 2024, $1 billion expected in 2026).

Rising Interest Expense: Debt has nearly doubled since 2021 to $22.2 billion currently. Interest expenses have increased from $569 million in 2021 to $1.1 billion in the trailing 12 months.

Competitive Moat

Lockheed has a wide competitive moat in my view, based on the following factors:

  • High barriers to entry - product complexity and massive R&D requirements create huge entry barriers for potential competitors
  • High switching costs for customers - once contracts are approved and in motion, switching to new suppliers is incredibly costly. Once contracts are approved, competition is all but locked out, as is the case with the F-35 program which is expected to drive revenues for LMT into the 2060s. LMT also benefits from decades of high-margin sustainment, modernization, and upgrade work.
  • Relationships with government decision makers - LMT has strong relationships with the US Defense Department and decision makers in international governments which take years to build.
  • Regulatory and security requirements create a structural moat, since access to classified programs, export-controlled technologies, and sensitive supply chains is limited to a small group of contractors.

Q3 Earnings Takeaways (Date: October 26, 2025)

Lockheed Martin delivered strong top-line performance in Q3 with 9% year-over-year revenue growth and record backlog of $179 billion. The MFC and Space segments are outperforming expectations while the RMS segment is underperforming. On the financial side, the company increased dividends for the 23rd straight year and approved additional share repurchases.

The company announced several new contracts awarded during the quarter:

  • $9.8 billion contract for production of ~2,000 PAC-3 interceptors

  • $9.5 billion contract for JASSM and LRASM (cruise missiles)

  • $10.9 billion contract for Sikorsky helicopters

  • Space backlog up to $38 billion

The company also delivered 46 F-35 aircraft during the quarter and projects between 175 and 190 deliveries in 2025. Since inception, the company has delivered 1,200 F-35s. There is a strong backlog in the F-35 program as well, for 265 aircraft as of the end of Q3, with another 151 orders in the first week of Q4.

A key risk arises from the company’s exposure to fixed-price development contracts. In 2024, the company recorded “reach-forward losses” of ~$2 billion, which continued into 2025 with losses of $1.8 billion in Aeronautics and $1.46 billion in MFC. These charges resulted in a 19.5% decline in EBIT and 22.9% drop in Net Income in 2024, with a further decline in net income to $3.7 billion in Q3 2025 vs. $4.8 billion in the prior period.

Financial Analysis

LMT's financials reveal a company grappling with profitability pressures despite impressive growth.

Historical Performance

  • Cost of Revenues increased 7.53% for the trailing twelve months vs. the period prior, while revenues only increased 2.9%

  • Net income is down 37% in TTM

  • Gross profit margin is down to 8.25% from 14% in 2019

  • Accounts receivable is 29.5% of total assets

  • Major liabilities are long-term debt ($20.5B) and unearned revenue ($10.3B).

The main thing that stands out in the financial statements is the long-term downward trend in gross profit margin. While revenues have grown by 81% since 2015, gross profit has only grown by 32%. The bulk of the margin compression has occurred after 2021, suggesting that it is likely a result of inflationary pressures on LMT’s raw materials and intermediate goods purchases.

For companies like LMT, inflation creates a mismatch between long-cycle fixed-price contracts and short-cycle price spikes in the supply chain. Inflation therefore leads to cost overruns on long term projects, since revenue is locked in years in advance while costs arise as materials are needed. However, the company is flushing those inefficiencies out of its system as costs stabilize, old contracts get written down, and new contracts are agreed.

Valuation

I use two different models to value LMT shares. First, I projected free cash flows starting with guidance this year of $6.6 billion in free cash flow and $74.6 billion in revenue. That means we're starting with a FCF to Sales ratio of 8.85%. In my base case I assumed Sales growth of 4% annually through 2027 and then 3% after that in perpetuity, in line with historical revenue growth. FCF to Sales margin I project expanding to 9.85% by 2030 to reflect margin expansion to historical norms (as a result of fixed-price contracts during the period of high inflation being completed). The table below shows the projected cash flows and their present values (in millions of dollars):

Year Sales FCF to Sales FCF PV
2025 $74,577.00 8.85% $6,600.00
2026 $77,560.08 9.05% $7,019.12 $6,540.76
2027 $80,662.48 9.25% $7,461.21 $6,478.89
2028 $83,082.36 9.45% $7,851.21 $6,352.92
2029 $85,574.83 9.65% $8,257.90 $6,226.61
2030 $88,142.07 9.85% $8,681.92 $6,100.19
Terminal Value $145,662.79 $102,347.30

WACC Calculation

I used a value of 0.6 for beta to approximately reflect the historical mean over the last 10 years. The 24 month beta is only 0.23, which I believe would give us an unrealistically low value for cost of equity. Using a value of 0.6 for beta assumes a mean reversion toward historical norms and gives us a cost of equity of 7.8%, which is reasonable for a fairly safe, non-cyclical dividend-paying defense stock. To get a 7.8% cost of equity with a beta of 0.6, I use a 6% equity risk premium and a 4.2% risk free rate, which are both fairly conservative values.

Cost of debt is approximated using $1.09 billion interest paid divided by $22.2 billion in total debt, multiplied by 1 - the effective tax rate of 14.5%. Using a weight of equity of 86% and weight of debt of 14%, we get a WACC of 7.3% for LMT.

Fair Value Per Share

Using the WACC of 7.3% to discount the projected cash flows back to present gives a firm value of $134 billion. Subtracting net debt from that gives an equity value of $115 billion. Finally, dividing by the shares outstanding of 231.4 million gives a fair value per share of $498.

Sensitivity Analysis

I modified the WACC, TG rate, and margin assumptions to try to determine the best and worst case scenarios in addition to my base case scenario, as shown in the table below:

Case WACC Terminal Growth Assumptions Fair Value Per Share
Bear 8.1% 2.50% No Margin Expansion $375
Base 7.3% 3% Modest Margin Expansion $498
Bull 6.8% 3.50% Strong Margin Expansion $739

The bear case using WACC of 8.1%, TG rate of 2.5%, and assuming FCF margins stay at 8.85% in perpetuity gives a fair value per share of $375. The bull case, using WACC of 6.8%, TG rate of 3.5%, and assuming FCF margins expand to 10.85% gives a fair value per share of $739. The bear case represents 36% downside, the base case suggests 14% downside, and the bull case suggests 27% upside.

Relative Valuation

To value LMT's shares on a relative basis, I examine the stock's historical valuation ratios, starting with Price to FCF:

My base case projection for 2030 gives a projected free cash flow of $8.7 billion. The chart above shows Price to FCF hovering around an average value of about 20x over the last 5 years, only recently increasing to close to 30x FCF. Applying a 20x FCF multiple based on projected FCF in 2030 gives an Equity value of $174 billion for 2030. Discounting that back to present at a cost of equity of 7.8% and dividing by number of outstanding shares gives a fair value per share of $515 - not too far off the base case FCF valuation of $498.

The charts below show PE and PS ratios averaging about 18 and 1.7, respectively:

Applying a 1.7 multiple to my projected 2030 sales gives an equity value of $150 billion and fair value per share of $445. Applying an 18x PE ratio to consensus 2030 earnings per share of $37.25 gives a fair value of $670.50 per share in 2030, which discounts to $461 today.

To recap, the estimated fair value for LMT shares based on each valuation method are shown in the table below:

Method Fair Share Value
DCF $498
P/FCF $515
P/E $461
P/S $445

The different valuation methods all show the same thing: LMT slightly overvalued compared to the most recent share price of $579.

Peer Comparison

The following table shows a key ratio comparison between Lockheed and its Big 5 competitors. Although Boeing's defense segment performance is currently distorted by commercial aerospace issues, I included it for the sake of completeness. Note that all ratios are based on non-GAAP, forward-looking values:

Metric LMT RTX NOC BA GD
P/E 26.56 32.62 25.59 N/A 23.85
P/S 1.81 3.11 2.27 2.31 1.91
P/B 23.79 4.12 6.00 N/A 4.03
EV/EBITDA 16.60 21.12 18.63 N/A 16.97
Dividend Yield 2.29% 1.32% 1.35% 0% 1.63%
SA Valuation Grade C- D- D C- D+

Generally speaking, LMT is cheaper than its peers based on relative valuation metrics. While GD and NOC have lower P/E ratios, and all competitors have superior price to book ratios, LMT has the best value in terms of price to sales and EV to EBITDA ratios. It also has the best Seeking Alpha Valuation Grade (C-) of the group, tied with BA.

These premium valuation multiples seem to be a result of a recent run up in share prices of the entire group of Big 5 defense contractors over the last two months. Every single one has outperformed the S&P 500, which is relatively flat. Boeing (BA) has had the biggest increase, up 29.8% over two months, while LMT, NOC, and RTX are all up double digits. GD is up a modest 5.1%, still more than doubling the S&P's return in the same time period. This explains why all 5 stocks earn valuations in the C- to D- range based on Seeking Alpha's methodology.

Stock Performance

Over the last 10 years, LMT has underperformed the S&P in total return. $10k invested in SPY would have grown to $43.5k, while $10k invested in LMT would have only grown to $35k. That still represents a very solid return for a low-beta stock though, and in some ways the recent momentum looks like LMT playing catch-up on the S&P 500.

However, LMT recently has tended to get ahead of itself, which is evident when comparing price behavior to the relative strength index. Spikes in the 14-day RSI have tended to precede near term highs in the stock price. Twice in the last 18 months has the RSI risen above 80, and in both cases it signaled a coming peak in the share price:

Now the RSI is at 78.88, getting close to the >80 range that marked prior highs. The stock is up nearly 33% in a little over a month, which is an unusually strong move for a low beta defensive stock like LMT, most likely attributable to Trump's push for a $1.5 trillion defense budget (which I view as an implausible scenario given the country's current $2 trillion budget deficit). Although I'm not a technical analyst, it wouldn't surprise me to see the stock test its prior all time high of $612 per share before Q4 earnings are reported on January 29th, 2026, which could then serve as a catalyst for either breaking out from its prior trading range or correcting to a more reasonable valuation.

At this stage I think a correction may be a more likely outcome given the premium valuation - any weakness in earnings could cause a near-term sell-off in my view.

What to Watch in Q4 Earnings

Looking ahead to Q4 earnings on January 29th, the key question I'll be looking for an answer to is whether profitability has finally bottomed. Declining values in reach-forward losses would be a good sign, while stationary or increasing losses would likely signal further erosion in profitability in the near term. A clean quarter on this front would support the thesis that the worst inflation-related cost overruns are now in the rearview mirror.

Cash flow is also crucial. Management guidance implied a strong fourth quarter free cash flow contribution. Any shortfall on this front could raise concerns about working capital intensity. Updates on pension funding requirements and interest expense trends will also matter, given the company's higher leverage relative to recent history.

Finally, I'll be looking at forward commentary in addition to headline numbers. Any signals that margin recovery is being pushed out or that new fixed-price development programs are being agreed under aggressive assumptions would challenge the valuation case at current prices. Conversely, evidence of improving contract discipline would strengthen the long-term investment thesis.

Conclusion

Whilst Lockheed Martin is a best-in-class defense contractor with a diversified portfolio of business segments, I believe the stock's recent run up has led it into overvalued territory. To add a disclaimer: I am a long-term shareholder of LMT and have no plans to buy or sell the stock in the near future. If the stock corrects toward the $450-500 range I would consider adding to my position.

If you like this analysis, follow me and stay tuned for updates! I plan to provide an update after Lockheed's earnings at the end of January, and also to publish initial valuation pieces on LMT's competitors like RTX, NOC, BA, and GD. Thank you for reading!

data.title: MoneyShow's Best Investment Ideas For 2026: Part 9
data.description:
MoneyShow’s top contributing analysts, strategists, and newsletter editors share their investment recommendations for the year ahead. See Part 8 here.

Target Corp.

By Ben Reynolds, Editor of Sure Dividend

Target Corp. (TGT) was founded in 1902, and after a failed bid to expand into Canada, has operations solely in the US market. Its business consists of about 1,850 big box stores, which offer general merchandise and food, as well as serving as distribution points for the company’s e-commerce business.

Target should produce more than $100 billion in revenue this year. The company also sports an impressive dividend increase streak of 57 years. Target’s impressive dividend record makes it a member of the elite Dividend Kings list.

The company’s payout ratio sat at 62% of expected earnings for 2025. While this isn’t a particularly low payout ratio, we view the dividend as safe, due in large part to Target’s competitive advantage and recession safety.

Target’s competitive advantage comes from its everyday low prices on attractive merchandise in its guest-friendly stores. The company is able to offer low prices thanks to its large scale. With that said, Target faces intense competition from other top-class retailers like Walmart (WMT), Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN), and Costco Wholesale Corp. (COST).

Target posted third-quarter earnings on Nov. 19, 2025, and results were slightly better than expected. Adjusted earnings per share came to $1.78, which was seven cents above estimates. Target has grown its EPS at an average annual rate of about 8% during the last decade. But we don’t expect the company to replicate this favorable result in the coming years. Overall, we expect 5% annualized growth from what should be a modest level for 2025.

Based on expected EPS of $7.30 in 2025, Target shares were recently trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of less than 13. Our target P/E ratio is 14. As a result, we believe target to be undervalued at current prices.

But what really stands out about Target stock is its high dividend yield of close to 5%. Target is one of the most high-quality stocks available with a yield approaching 5%. Investors can buy Target stock now, lock in high yields, expect dividend growth in coming years, and wait for valuation multiple expansion.

The Trade Desk Inc.

By Bret Kenwell, US Investment Analyst at eToro

Last year wasn’t an easy one for The Trade Desk Inc. (TTD). But with the shares recently below $40, the risk/reward looks far different than when the stock traded above $140.

After a strong election year fueled TTD’s growth, bulls entered 2025 with confidence as shares finished 2024 higher by more than 60%. A year later, the script had flipped, with TTD shedding roughly two-thirds of its value and even taking out its 2022 bear market lows.

The Trade Desk operates as a buy-side advertising technology platform enabling marketers to plan, execute, and optimize programmatic campaigns across display, video, CTV, audio, and mobile. A tough year of comps in 2025, paired with hiccups in its main platform update, weighed on results - a spinout it couldn’t correct as year-end approached.

Still, there were notable positives. Management emphasized that the platform delays were non-negotiable, as they prioritized product quality over short-term optics. As co-founder and CEO Jeff Green noted, “In Q4 [2024] there were a series of decisions we could have made to enhance the short-term performance... We consistently choose to focus on the long term opportunity.”

The C-suite also expanded, adding a much-needed COO with Vivek Kundra, CRO Anders Mortensen (from Alphabet), and CFO Alex Kayyal (from Salesforce), positioning the team to streamline operations and better execute long-term goals.

Financial performance improved as well. While February’s Q4 report delivered a slight revenue miss and slightly below consensus guidance, subsequent quarters saw revenue and guidance exceed expectations.

Consensus expectations call for GAAP earnings growth of about 29% in 2026 and 26% in 2027 (with adjusted earnings growth of 16.5% and 13.3%). Analysts expect revenue growth of about 16% in both years, and free cash flow growth of 15.5% in 2026 and 20% in 2027.

If TTD meets these metrics, the stock appears compelling at less than 18 times 2026 earnings estimates, not to mention forward earnings and FCF multiples near trough levels (shown in chart). Bulls should feel further reassurance as the company operates with a pristine balance sheet.

Recommended Action: Buy TTD.

UFP Technologies Inc.

By Doug Gerlach, Editor of SmallCap Informer

UFP Technologies Inc. (UFPT) is a high-quality growth investment, currently trading in an attractive valuation range and offering compelling long-term prospects - supported by its mission-critical role as a custom development and manufacturing organization (CDMO) in the resilient medical device sector.

The investment thesis rests on the company’s proven ability to achieve significant growth through both organic expansion and disciplined strategic acquisitions, all while maintaining superior operational efficiency. The fundamental strength of UFP Technologies is evidenced by its accelerating growth and deeply entrenched market position.

The company surpassed $500 million in sales for the first time in 2024 and achieved annualized sales growth of 15.1% and EPS growth of 24.9% over the last decade. Recent performance shows continued momentum, with revenues soaring 37.2% in Q2 2025 compared to the prior-year period.

UFP focuses on high-growth, niche medical segments, such as single-use medical devices, sterile packaging, and robotic surgery, which itself is growing at a mid-teens compound annual growth rate (CAGR). Its status as a valued outsource partner to 26 of the top 30 largest medical device manufacturers provides sticky, predictable, long-term revenue streams.

Operationally, UFP boasts excellent quality metrics. The company achieved a Gross Margin of 29.1% in 2024, above the five-year average of 25.5%, demonstrating efficiency gained through its low-cost country manufacturing strategy (Mexico, Dominican Republic, Costa Rica) and successful integration of acquisitions.

The balance sheet remains robust, characterized by low debt (32% debt to total capital, trending down) and significant liquidity. That enables management to confidently execute its growth strategy, targeting 15% annual EPS growth over the next five years.

From a valuation standpoint, UFP is attractively priced. The shares currently sit firmly in our Buy Zone. With a highly favorable valuation, the stock offers a projected total annual return of 16.5%. Furthermore, the risk is mitigated by a strong 3.1-to-1 upside-to-downside ratio, well above the required minimum threshold. That suggests that the upside potential significantly outweighs the possible risk of loss at the current price.

The combination of robust, compounding growth in high-value niche markets and a conservative, efficient financial structure strongly supports buying UFP Technologies for long-term portfolio returns.

Recommended Action: Buy UFPT.

USA Compression Partners LP

By Martin Fridson, Editor of Forbes/Fridson Income Securities Investor

At $24.60 recently, USA Compression Partners LP (USAC) had an indicated annualized yield of 8.5%. We consider USAC a Buy up to $30. This Master Limited Partnership (MLP) is suitable for medium- to high-risk taxable portfolios.

For the 12 months ending November 2025, USAC delivered a 14.8% total return, handily outperforming the Alerian MLP Index’s 11.5%. USAC is a third-party provider of compression equipment and services to customers in the domestic oil and gas industry. Its compression equipment gives customers the ability to move natural gas through the domestic pipeline system.

The MLP also provides compression applications that assist in the production of crude oil. Energy Transfer LP (ET) owns the noneconomic general partnership interest in USAC and 46.9% of its outstanding common units.

Operations focus primarily on high-horsepower applications. These make up more than 75% of USAC’s active compression fleet. The compression fleet is spread over a diverse geographic area, covering five different basins. Those basins enjoy some of the industry’s most favorable economics, displaying resilience during periods of stressed pricing.

In 3Q 2025, USAC beat the analysts’ adjusted EBITDA consensus estimate, posting a record $160 million. Revenue from contract operations revenue rose by 5% year-over-year. In conjunction with the earnings release, USAC raised its full-year EBITDA projection by about 2.5%, exceeding Wall Street expectations at the time. The MLP benefited from a tight market for its compression services, recording a 94% average horsepower utilization rate.

As for USAC’s financial strength, Moody’s long-term corporate family rating stands at Ba3 with a Stable outlook. Standard & Poor’s long-term local issuer rating is B+ with a Stable outlook. On Oct. 28, 2025, Fitch Ratings reaffirmed its BB long-term default rating, maintaining its Stable outlook and deeming USAC’s leverage appropriate for the rating.

Positives cited by the rating agency included the MLP’s size, geographic diversity, strong customer retention history, and long-term relationships with major counterparties. Stable cash flows are a function of 100% fixed-fee, take-or-pay contracts that have no short-term volumetric or commodity price-based revenue risk. Most of the MLP’s revenue derives from large investment-grade counterparties.

Verizon Communications Inc.

By Kenny Polcari, Chief Market Strategist at SlateStone Wealth

In a market defined by volatility, shifting narratives, and rapid rotations between growth and value, Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) stands out as one of the most dependable, long-term, safe haven names available to investors.

It will never be confused with a high-flying tech stock. But Verizon offers something arguably more valuable for those seeking stability: predictable cash flows, strong dividend income, and a business model built on essential services that consumers and enterprises simply cannot live without.

Verizon operates like a modern utility. Wireless connectivity has become as essential as electricity, and that dependence creates a durable revenue stream insulated from economic cycles. Whether the economy is expanding, slowing, or treading water, people still pay their phone bill. That consistency allows Verizon to generate significant free cash flow, support its balance sheet, and maintain one of the most attractive dividends in the S&P 500 Index (SPX).

For long-term investors, it’s all about the dividend. Verizon’s yield regularly sits in the 6-7% range, supported by healthy cash coverage and nearly two decades of annual increases. When reinvested, this dividend becomes a compounding engine - steadily building position size and total return even in years when the stock price barely moves.

Over the long term, reinvested dividends can represent the majority of the investment’s total return, transforming a slow-growing stock into a reliable wealth-builder. Verizon also offers defensive characteristics that tend to shine during periods of uncertainty.

The company’s scale, network reach, and deep enterprise relationships create high barriers to entry, while the essential nature of wireless communication keeps subscriber churn low and cash flows steady. In choppy markets, that combination of yield, predictability, and resilience becomes highly attractive.

In short, Verizon is not a growth story - it’s a stability story. For investors seeking a long-term safe haven with dependable income and low volatility, Verizon checks all the boxes.

Recommended Action: Buy VZ.

Wave Life Sciences Ltd.

By John McCamant, editor of Medical Technology Stock Letter

We are recommending Wave Life Sciences Ltd. (WVE) as our more aggressive biotech Top Pick for 2026. The company is an emerging leader in discovery and drug development based on their pioneering best-in-class RNA chemistry.

The proprietary PRISM platform leverages advanced chemistry to optimize the pharmacological properties of oligonucleotides, enabling multi-modal approaches, including RNA editing, silencing, and splicing, to pursue genetically validated targets.

The company recently delivered a significant catalyst with proof-of-concept clinical data from WVE-007 for obesity, with additional data due in Q1 2026 and Q2 2026. In our view, the positive “muscle sparing” data for ‘007 has provided proof of principle for WVE’s proprietary PRISM platform and a favorable read-through for the company’s pipeline.

Wave’s recently released strong initial obesity data in the INLIGHT trial for WVE-007 (INHBE siRNA), which ushers in a new player in the obesity landscape. Based on this outstanding obesity data, the body “muscle sparing” profile validates INHBE as a novel mechanism of action (MOA) and also de-risks Wave’s RNAi platform.

WVE-007 (240 mg single dose) delivered excellent data with a 9.2% placebo-adjusted reduction in visceral fat accompanied by a statistically significant 0.9% gain in lean mass, which as initial PoC data are extremely encouraging. Importantly, the minimal total weight reduction (-0.9% placebo-adjusted) was driven by the loss of fat being offset by the gain in muscle. This creates a clear differentiation from GLP-1s.

Furthermore, the durability data, 75%-78% Activin E silencing maintained through Day 85, confirms the viability of a once-or-twice-yearly dosing regimen, a major advantage for WVE’s RNA silencing platform. WVE-007 showed no GI adverse events and no discontinuations.

With the novel MOA de-risked, we can expect significant M&A speculation as Big Pharma players with GLP-1 assets like Novo Nordisk (NVO) - which just lost a bidding war with Pfizer Inc. (PFE) over MTSR - look to secure this novel “muscle-sparing” drug with once-or-twice-per-year dosing potential.

WVE is planning for a Phase II development program testing WVE-007 both as a monotherapy and as an add-on to GLP-1s in both induction and maintenance settings in 2026. In addition, WVE remains on track to report six-month follow-up data from the 240mg cohort and three-month data from the 400mg cohort in Q1 2026.

In our view, the next two data readouts for ‘007 at higher doses in Q1 2026 and Q2 2026 have the potential to serve as significant catalysts for the stock.

Recommended Action: Buy WVE.

Willdan Group Inc.

By Tracey Ryniec, Senior Stock Strategist at Zacks Investment Research

Willdan Group, Inc. (WLDN) is a hidden AI Revolution stock with the electric grid in the spotlight. The company is a nationwide provider of professional, technical, and consulting services to utilities, government agencies, and private industries.

WLDN is a smaller company with a market cap of $1.7 billion. Willdan’s specialties include electric grid solutions, energy efficiency and sustainability, engineering and planning, and municipal financial consulting. As of November 2025, it had active projects in 42 states with 50-plus offices in North America.

Studies have shown that there could be a 50% increase in US electricity demand by 2050. Much of it will be from data centers that will power AI, robots, and self-driving cars. In powering data centers, Willdan has capabilities across the project, from project management through to engineering, then to policy, forecasting, and data analytics.

It also has active M&A to add capabilities where demand is strong. In 2025, it bought Alternative Power Generation, which had $37 million in revenue. It does grid interconnection solutions from concept to concrete. Its clients are data centers, commercial, and renewable developers.

Willdan is currently doing a grid impact study for the State of Virginia. Virginia has the largest data center market in the country. Questions about the grid are rising. Willdan’s expertise is in grid modernization.

Willdan has been raising full-year revenue and earnings guidance throughout the year as business remained strong. Net revenue grew 26.8% for the year through the third quarter of 2025. Earnings are expected to jump 70% in 2025 and another 9.7% in 2026 on strong margin growth. The Zacks Consensus is looking for $4.13 for 2025, up from $2.43 in 2024.

The stock soared to all-time highs in 2025, but valuations are still attractive. Willdan trades with a forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 26. Compared to other AI stocks, that P/E ratio looks cheap. It also has a price-to-sales (P/S) ratio of 2.5, which is low compared to others in the AI Revolution space.

The data center buildout and grid optimization have only just begun. They should keep Willdan busy for 2026 and beyond.

Recommended Action: Buy WLDN.

Williams Companies Inc.

By Tom Hutchinson, editor of Cabot Dividend Investor

Natural gas is by far the cleanest burning fossil fuel. It is also by far the fastest growing fossil fuel. That’s why I recommend Williams Companies Inc. (WMB), a firm involved in the transmission, gathering, processing, and storage of natural gas.

Clean energy is not yet cheap or reliable enough with the necessary infrastructure to replace the abundant and cheaper fossil fuel sources - and it probably won’t be for a long time. Even some of the staunchest alternative energy advocates increasingly see natural gas as a necessary transition to a clean energy future and a crucial grid stabilizer.

Demand across the globe is booming and continues to increase. At the same time, the US is the world’s largest producer of natural gas and the largest exporter. Natural gas use was already growing. The addition of soaring electricity demand and exploding US exports will continue to spike demand even more.

Williams operates the large Transco and Northwest pipeline systems that transport gas to densely populated areas from the Gulf to the East Coast. Roughly 30% of the natural gas in the US moves through Williams’ systems.

Midstream energy companies are not reliant on prices, but rather charge a fee for the storage, processing, and transport of oil and gas. These companies benefit from volumes and should benefit as more oil and gas sloshes around the country.

The overwhelming bulk of earnings are guaranteed by long-term contracts, too. And those contracts have automatic inflation adjustments built in. WMB also operates a near monopoly in its operating areas - so it doesn’t have to compete on price with other similar companies.

Williams currently pays a dividend of $2 per share annually, which translated to a solid 3.2% yield at the recent price. WMB has also increased the payout for the last nine consecutive years.

Recommended Action: Buy WMB.

W.W. Grainger Inc.

By Martin Fridson, Editor of Forbes/Fridson Income Securities Investor

The W.W. Grainger Inc. (GWW) 4.45% senior notes due 10/1/2035 are rated A2 with a Positive outlook by Moody’s and A+ with a Stable outlook by Standard & Poor’s. These securities are recommended for low-risk, tax-deferred portfolios. Interest payments are taxed as ordinary income.

This issue trades over the counter and can be bought or sold using the issue’s CUSIP number 384802AF1. At 99.07 recently, it had a 4.49% current yield and a 4.56% yield to maturity. That price compares with a 52-week high of $100.181. The notes are acceptable for purchase at $103.75 or lower.

W.W. Grainger is a worldwide distributor of industrial and safety supplies. It operates in two business segments, High-Touch Solutions (HTS) and Endless Assortment (EA), its online service. HTS caters to mid-size and large customers, offering approximately two million maintenance, repair, and operating products and services. The company’s product mix includes Safety and Security, Material Handling and Storage, Pumps, Plumbing and Test Equipment, Cleaning and Maintenance, and more.

In raising its outlook from Stable to Positive in February 2025, Moody’s highlighted Grainger’s strong business profile and atypically high operating margin for a distribution company. The rating agency also noted the company’s strong free cash flow generation and low financial leverage.

An additional positive cited by Moody’s was Grainger’s leading position within the fragmented market for industrial maintenance, repair, and operating (MRO) supplies. Also, said the rating agency, the company has a reputation for superior order fulfillment, complemented by its broad product offering and diverse customers and end-markets.

Moody’s further commented that it expected that Grainger would maintain its conservative financial policy, thereby keeping its debt/EBITDA below 1.5 times during periods of weak demand or macroeconomic pressures that might be brought about by tariffs. Grainger’s ability to navigate through trade conflicts, said Moody’s, is enhanced by pricing leverage, initiatives in productivity and efficiency, a sizable supplier base, and management’s exploration of alternative sourcing for certain products.

In addition, profit margins, cash flow, and other credit metrics during rough periods were likely to remain at levels that would support a credit upgrade. In the most recently reported quarter, Grainger’s sales modestly exceeded analysts’ expectations.

Recommended Action: Buy GWW’s 4.45% senior notes due 10/1/2035.

Originally posted on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boeing: Why Production, Not Valuation, Is The Real Story Now
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) stock has gained 1.9% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500. This report, however, does not serve to highlight the stock performance but discusses the December orders and deliveries as the company’s progress on sustainably hiking production is key to driving sustainable cash flow growth to clean up the balance sheet. Higher share prices are nice, but the quality of cash flow growth and ultimately share price growth is what matters, and that all can be traced back to airplane production.

Boeing Order Inflow Driven by Alaska Airlines Order

For this report, I'm using a tool developed by The Aerospace Forum to visualize airplane orders, deliveries, cancellations, and associated values.

Boeing ended the year on a strong note, booking 175 orders compared to 164 orders in the prior month. Orders consisted of 140 single-aisle airplanes and 35 wide-body airplanes valued at $13.4 billion:

  • Alaska Airlines (ALK) ordered 105 Boeing 737 MAX 10 airplanes and five Boeing 787-9s.
  • The US Navy ordered 10 Boeing 737-800A airplanes, which serve as maritime surveillance airplanes.
  • Ethiopian Airlines ordered 11 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • TUI Travel Aviation Finance Limited ordered two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer placed an order for 12 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer placed an order for five Boeing 777X airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer placed an order for 22 Boeing 787-9 airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer placed an order for three Boeing 787-9 airplanes.

During the month, the following changes were made to the order book:

  • Aviation Capital Group cancelled orders for four Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • An unidentified customer cancelled orders for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • TUI Travel Aviation Finance Limited cancelled orders for two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • A business jet customer cancelled an order for one Boeing 787-8.
  • CDB Leasing, China Southern Airlines, and Donghai Airlines were each identified as the customers for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • Southwest Airlines was identified as the customer for two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Qatar Airways was identified as the customer for five Boeing 777Fs.
  • All Nippon Airways converted orders for one Boeing 787-10 to one Boeing 787-9.
  • Lufthansa selected GE engines for seven Boeing 787-9s on order.
  • China Eastern Airlines was identified as the customer for two Boeing 787-9s; the order was previously listed as two orders for the Boeing 787-10.
  • United Airlines converted orders for 56 Boeing 787-9s to orders for 56 Boeing 787-10s.

Order inflow in December leaned heavily towards single-aisle airplanes driven by the order from Alaska Airlines. However, we are also seeing continued interest in the Boeing 787 and Boeing 777X and some conversion activity from the Boeing 787-9 to the Boeing 787-10, which should bring Boeing better margins. In total, 175 airplanes were ordered, and eight orders were cancelled, bringing the net orders to 167 airplanes valued at $12.9 billion. In the same month last year, Boeing booked seven net orders valued at $2.6 billion.

In 2025, Boeing booked 1,175 orders and 100 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 1,075 units valued at $114.6 billion. In the prior year, Boeing booked 569 orders and 192 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 377, valued at $33 billion.

ASC 606 adjustments decreased by seven units. This was driven by a decrease of 16 units on the Boeing 777 program, a decrease of one unit on the Boeing 787 program partially offset by an increase of 10 units on the Boeing 737 program. During the month, orders that were already doubtful were indeed cancelled, leading to a reduction in the adjustment tally.

ASC 606 adjustments might be a vague term. Therefore, I have provided a description form prior analyses about what they are:

ASC 606 adjustments are corrections to the backlog for orders where a purchase contract exists, but additional criteria are not met. A decrease in ASC 606 adjustments can be achieved when the order, in fact, does get cancelled or the additional criteria are met.

Boeing ended the year with a total of 590 orders in the ASC 606 adjustment category, which covers 8.8% of the backlog. As previously stated, it does not mean that roughly 9% of the backlog is necessarily prone to cancellation, but for 9% of the backlog, additional criteria are not yet met. Occasionally, this leads to cancellations.

Boeing Airplane Deliveries Reach New High for 2025

In December, Boeing delivered 63 airplanes, consisting of 45 single-aisle airplanes and 18 wide-body airplanes valued at $5 billion:

  • Boeing delivered 45 Boeing 737 airplanes consisting of one Boeing 737-800A and 45 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered two Boeing 767s, consisting of one Boeing 767-2C used as a baseline for the tanker airplane and one freighter.
  • Boeing delivered two 777F airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered a total of 14 Boeing 787 airplanes, consisting of 12 -9 models and two -10 models.

Sequentially, Boeing’s deliveries increased sharply, driven by higher deliveries on the Boeing 737 MAX and Boeing 787 programs, which also are the company’s profitable airplanes. The increase is driven by a combination of clearing inventories and benefiting from higher production rates. In the same month last year, Boeing delivered only 30 airplanes valued at $2.7 billion, as the company’s production rates were lower and affected by a strike.

In 2025, Boeing delivered 600 airplanes valued at $45.6 billion compared to 348 deliveries valued at $26.1 billion a year ago. The delivery total for 2025 was substantially higher but was around 25 airplanes lower than I had targeted.

The book-to-bill ratio, an indicator of supply-demand health, was 2.8x in terms of orders and 2.7x in terms of value. The ratio for the month reflects substantial order inflow as customers are ordering airplanes with Boeing after years of weaker order inflow. For 2025, the book-to-bill ratio in terms of units was 2x and 2.7x measured by value.

Conclusion: Boeing Full-Year Shows a Sustainable Growth Path

Boeing is definitely not where it should be yet, but we note that full-year deliveries were at the highest level since 2018, when Boeing delivered over 800 jets. I believe this serves as a layup for Boeing to deliver around 700 jets this year. January delivery figures tend to be weak, but on the Boeing 737 MAX program we should see the benefits of a production rate of 42 airplanes per month and an additional rate increase to 47 airplanes per month later in the year, while the Boeing 787 program is expected to transition from 8 to 10 airplanes per month. The output will be dependent on the timing of rate increases; if they come extremely late in the year, the deliveries could be closer to 660-670.

We note that demand remains robust, and orders that are currently being placed support production rates that are twice as high as the current production rates. 2026 is likely going to be a year with growth being less robust. That is driven by a work stoppage in 2024, which gave Boeing an easier comp in 2025 while the company had an inventory of airplanes to deliver. That inventory is mostly gone by now, meaning that from here we see a cleaner growth path driven by production rate increases. Boeing will likely be one or two years late on getting back to annual production of 800+ airplanes, but we see a more credible and sustainable path forward for the company to increase production and deleverage the balance sheet.

I believe that instead of looking at valuation, what matters now is looking at how the production develops. A sustainable growth path for production will ultimately drive cash flow and provide more predictable support for the company's share price.

data.title: FAST Growth But High Priced: Fastenal Remains A Hold Following Q4 2025 Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my first article on Fastenal (FAST), I highlighted the company’s continued success in expanding sales to big-ticket customers and gaining wallet and market share as the keys to being a successful investment. FAST continues to win with its largest customer cohort: those with $50k/month in revenue or more. The number of on-sites in the 50k+ category grew 14.0% YoY in Q4, showing market share gains, and the average monthly sales for this group grew 3.8% in Q4, showing wallet share gains. This combination drove an incredible 18.3% growth in net sales from its largest customers. This highlights where the compounding potential is for Fastenal. Sales to larger customer is a key strategy for sustained organic revenue growth and operating margin expansion.

At the time of my last article on FAST, the stock’s valuation was too rich for me, and it’s time to look again now that the company has reported Q4 2025 earnings. The growth story only looks stronger, as momentum has improved since Q125. With the stock down since that article, is it time to buy now or wait for a better entry point?

Q4 2025 Earnings Review

Key accounts activity and contract wins, and the stickiness of FMI and digital channels, powered FAST to double-digit sales growth and operating margin expansion as it continues to capture market and wallet share gains despite sluggish manufacturing and industrial output. The key driver is the shift to larger customers, which effectively increases FAST’s quality of revenue.

Daily sales rate growth of 11.0% and net sales growth of 11.1% were impressive from a business perspective, but failed to offset the headwinds from both the industry and the market selloff related to geopolitical concerns of President Trump’s desire and veiled threats to take over Greenland and the responding increase of tariffs to EU nations.

A decline in gross margin and continued uncertainty around the recovery in manufacturing and industrial production across its end markets remain items to watch.

Gross margin (GM) contracted 50 bps to 44.3% in Q4, and by 10 bps to 45.0% for FY25. Negative price/cost impacts, the timing of higher inventory sell-through, fewer supplier rebates than normal, and the shift to larger customer contract sales negatively impacted GM, while the expansion of the fastener line undertaken in 2025 benefited GM. Management signaled that some of the decline is temporary rather than structural. However, the company is purposely sacrificing some gross margin for better operating leverage by shifting towards larger sales accounts, so operating margin and leverage are better indicators of the company’s financial strength, and continued GM degradation is acceptable

FAST’s operating leverage was once again seen in Q4, with SG&A to sales coming in 50 bps lower YoY (25.4% in Q425 vs. 25.9% in Q424). While the company saw higher variable pay (bonus) growth than sales in the quarter, base pay grew at a slower rate, and the shift towards larger customers, onsite sales, and FMI device sales drove a separation from occupancy growth and sales growth. While Q4 sales grew 11.1% YoY, occupancy costs only rose 6.4%. During Q2 2026, the increase in variable comp will be lapped, and some leverage should be gained at that level of the operating expenses. In 2025, FAST shared about 29% of incremental earnings via incentive comp expansion; only one quarter of that drag rolls into 2026. This implies better incrementals if sales growth holds.

This operating leverage, built through efficiencies of scale and technology, is a key part of my investment thesis in FAST. The key metrics to look for, beyond operating margin expansion, are growth in new contract signings (contract count increased 7.2% YoY in Q4), and continued strength in sales growth from customers that generate 50k in sales or more per month. $50k+ sites grew 14.0% YoY, and sales to this group rose 18.3%. Contract signings offer the potential for high sales growth, as these are national, regional, and local multi-site customers with significant revenue potential. These accounts have been growing rapidly since Q124 (+20.4% two-year stack), and the sales attributed to them are beginning to flow into the business today.

In the chart above, a lag between contract count and DSR acceleration is evident. Making continued growth in this metric a key to sustained organic revenue growth.

Looking Ahead

While not providing guidance, when asked about the outlook for 2026, Daniel Florness cited strong momentum as a source of confidence in delivering FY26 double-digit net sales growth. That confidence was backed up when analyst Ryan Merkel asked if a high-twenties incremental margin is reasonable to expect from the company once it laps its variable comp increase anniversary.

This commentary gives us a simple way to model growth and see if FAST justifies the current 39x trailing P/E multiple it holds, or even a lower multiple.

Valuation

To determine whether FAST is buyable given the market's current expectations for its growth, I have built a 5-year exit multiple model assuming a 5-year compounded sales growth rate of 11.0% and an incremental operating margin of 27.5%, consistent with the potential high-twenties incremental discussed during the earnings call. The model assumes no share buybacks, which could provide a catalyst for EPS growth. It also assumes robust sales growth and the maintenance of a healthy incremental margin profile without any down years. This model is not an explicit forecast, just an expectations exercise.

If Fastenal can deliver an 11.0% net sales CAGR, 27.5% incremental margin, and maintain a 75% rate of operating income to net income conversion, the stock could deliver a roughly 8.3% CAGR if the exit multiple is 30x TTM P/E. That return increases to an 11.7% CAGR if FAST trades at 35x after 5 years. Adding the current 2.1% yield which might increase over 5 years, these assumptions equate to a 10.4% total return CAGR, and a 13.8% total return CAGR, respectively.

This level of uninterrupted growth seems optimistic and leaves me feeling like FAST is still a stock to keep an eye on, rather than to buy at this point. If your opinion differs significantly one way or another from these illustrative assumptions, it might push the stock to a buy or sell.

Risks

Gross margin headwinds must be carefully considered with FAST. While I typically do not like seeing GM declining. FAST’s GM is both superior to its main rivals, W.W. Grainger (GWW) and MSC Industrial Direct (MSM), and on a similar trajectory of gradually declining. However, this is by design, as FAST shifts its sales strategy towards grabbing market share and embedding itself within the operations of larger customers, it will sacrifice gross margin for SG&A and operating leverage. Operating margin expansion is the key here.

There were some potentially temporary headwinds to GM in Q4 that are expected to normalize. Higher COGS and lower supplier rebates are expected to normalize, and benefit GM, while the positive impact from the fastener expansion project and continued shift to large customers will negatively impact GM. If this outlook deteriorates and negatively impacts operating margin, the growth thesis may be threatened or broken.

Manufacturing weakness also remains a risk, and is likely muting growth for FAST while it takes market share and expands its reach into larger contract customers.

This could act as a catalyst that could either boost sales growth rates or act as a buffer if market share gains normalize to a lower level.

Conclusion

FAST continues to outperform its end markets, and drive growth in its main KPIs and key drivers of the business.

Fastenal’s business momentum continues to move in the right direction. The company is successfully executing on its most important long-term strategy, shifting its sales mix toward larger, stickier customers and benefiting from clear operating leverage despite a sluggish industrial backdrop. Growth in $50k+ monthly customers, rising wallet share, expanding on-site penetration, and accelerating contract signings all reinforce the quality and durability of FAST’s business.

While the fundamental story has improved and the company remains a highly investable business, the stock's valuation still demands near-flawless execution. My scenario analysis and expectations model shows that even under optimistic assumptions, including sustained double-digit sales growth and high-twenties incremental margins, expected total shareholder returns appear modest unless FAST can maintain a premium multiple over the next five years.

With little margin for error in the stock, and the uncertainty for industrial and manufacturing demand for the business, I rate FAST stock a hold until the valuation becomes cheaper or manufacturing PMI increases and supports more robust sales growth.

data.title: Truist: Muted Growth Overshadows Buyback Plans (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining just about 3%. While the company has one of the most compelling geographic footprints across the Southeast, growth has been lackluster. While the company also faced sizable unrealized losses on its securities portfolio, capital is now in a better position, enabling a focus on shareholder returns. I last covered shares of Truist in October, upgrading them to a Buy, and that recommendation has played out well with the stock gaining 14% since then. Aided by improved market sentiment around regional banks, shares have even eclipsed my $47.50 price target.

On Wednesday, TFC reported a solid Q4 but offered cautious guidance, sending shares down 2% in early trading. Given this performance and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit TFC.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Truist earned $1.12, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue grew 4% to $5.3 billion. Frankly, this quarter was a continuation of themes we have seen in prior quarters. While lending growth was strong, its deposit accumulation remains comparably weak. Credit metrics were mixed, though reserves are strong. While revenue growth has been soft, a shrinking AOCI loss has enabled more capital returns. In other words, the picture is mixed with genuine positives but also clear soft spots.

Muted deposit growth offsets solid loan growth

One repeated disappointment for me has been the company’s modest deposit growth. This continued in Q1 with deposits falling by $1 billion sequentially to $396 billion. This was up just 1.5% from last year. Over the past year, deposit costs are down 25bps, and its cumulative beta to Fed rate cuts is 30%, at the low end of the 35-45% I like to see. The fact that TFC has been cutting rates comparatively little and still struggling to generate deposit growth is disappointing, and I struggle to see this accelerating much in 2026 given muted industrywide deposit growth. This difficulty in adding deposits limits potential balance sheet growth.

Turning to the other side of its balance sheet, loans ended the quarter up 1.5% to $329 billion. This was up a strong 7% from last year. It has been a difficult environment for banks to generate loan growth, so this was a very strong performance, reflecting TFC’s focus on business banking where trends have been stronger than in commercial real estate (“CRE”). CRE is just 7% of TFC’s loan portfolio, an unusually small share, which I view positively given ongoing difficulties in that sector. Given Fed rate cuts, loan yields fell by 13bps sequentially to 5.87%. I am cautious on the ability to sustain this level of loan growth without an improvement in deposit trends.

Net interest income was $3.75 billion, and its net interest margin (NIM) of 3.07% was flat from a year ago. NIM should be helped by ongoing fixed rate maturities, though the benefit will be comparatively small in 2026. For instance, while its total securities portfolio yields 2.88%, maturities in 2026 are higher yielding at 3.76%. There should still be some yield pick-up as TFC should be able to reinvest over 4%, but the benefit will be smaller. Similarly, fixed rate loans have a higher than average yield at 6.4%. As these portfolios fully mature and roll over, there will be a benefit to net interest income, but most of this will occur in 2027-2029, not 2026.

Elsewhere, noninterest income was up 5% to $1.5 billion, led by a 28% jump in investment banking fees. While this is a fairly small part of TFC’s business, M&A activity is picking up, which I expect to continue through 2026. Thank to higher markets, wealth management fees also grew 6% to $365 million, and I expect continued growth here in 2026 with Q1 likely to be sequentially stronger as fees are charged in arrears.

Credit quality and capital are solid but not excellent

Turning to credit quality, charge-offs were 57bps in Q4. This was down 2bps from last year but still a somewhat elevated level. For the full year, charge-offs were 54bps, down 5bs from 2025. TFC set aside $512 million for credit losses, up from the $471 million provisioned a year ago, reflecting the still elevated level of net charge-offs. Truist has 48bps of nonperforming loans, a level it has been around for over year (except for a temporary dip in Q2). With $5 billion of allowances for losses, TFC has about 3x coverage of NPLs, which is a healthy level that should limit the need to materially increase reserves even in a modest economic slowdown.

Truist ended the quarter with a tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1) of 10.8%, down from 11.5% last year given its capital returns in 2025. If we were to include the unrealized losses in AOCI, capital would be closer to 9.5% from 9.7% last year. I like to see TFC maintain an adjusted CET1 of 9.5-10%, so the bank is operating inside this range, albeit it at the low end. Importantly, we should see adjusted capital improve this year as bonds pull to par and mature, reducing the unrealized AOCI loss.

Slow growth increases the focus on capital returns

Shares offer a 4.2% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure given its capital position. In Q4, the company bought back $750 million of stock, and it plans for $1 billion of repurchases in Q1. Thanks to buybacks, its share count was down about 3.7% from last year. In December, Truist also authorized a $10 billion share repurchase program. This program has no end date and will likely last for several years.

Alongside results, TFC issued guidance for 2026, targeting 4-5% revenue growth whereas I was hoping for at least 5%. Expenses are expected to rise about 1.75%, a bit higher than anticipated. We also are unlikely to see any improvement in net charge-offs, which is a bit disappointing. TFC’s lack of CRE exposure means its NPLs did not rise as early in the cycle, but it also is likely to see later improvement in credit metrics than some peers.

Back in October, I was targeting $4.20-$4.45 in 2026 EPS, which assumed $2 billion of share repurchases. I now see the company earning $4.25-$4.45 with $4 billion of buybacks. Essentially, a lower share count offsets somewhat lower interest income growth. Over the past few months, we have also seen sentiment around regional banks improve, lifting multiples across the sector and making my 11x price target of $47.50 appear a bit more conservative than it previously did.

Shares are a suitable hold in portfolios

Between buybacks and dividends, TFC should have a ~10% capital return in 2026, higher than I expected as management signals comfort with running capital a bit lower than I anticipated. This level of capital return can support shares. That said, slow deposit growth and persistently higher charge-offs limit multiple expansion in my view. I struggle to see shares getting past 12x or $52 given these negatives.

Given capital returns, I do not see shares as a Sell by any means, but after their strong rally, I believe much of the upside has been captured with returns likely to be closer to 8% than 15% over the next 12 months. As such, I am moving shares to a Hold. With solid capital returns and little CRE exposure, TFC is a suitable hold in portfolios, especially for income-oriented investors. That said, I do not see enough upside to continue buying TFC shares here and would like a pullback to $46 before adding.

data.title: Here's How Not To Invest $100,000 In 2026
data.description:
Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing - Warren Buffett

Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

There's a million and one ways to lose money. But retail investors seem to make the same mistakes over and over again.

Recently I've been reflecting on the Buffett quote which I opened this article with.

In 2025, I relocated from Bali, Indonesia, to Dubai in the UAE. And there's a lot of young kids, driving fast cars (mostly rentals).

Testosterone and bravado are sky high, and I can't stop but think to myself that they have no clue whatsoever what they're doing.

It's not the fast car that introduces the risk, it's the driver.

While I wouldn't climb a mountain free solo because of the risk to my life, Alex Honnold can climb that mountain and we accept that there is less risk to his life than there is to mine.

In investing it's the same. If you fail to acquire the basic skills which are required to perform decently, you will do poorly.

Thankfully, often in investing, not making bad decisions is at least as important as making good ones.

So if you have $100,000 that you want to allocate to equities, in particular if you're a dividend investor, here are a handful of blunders you want to avoid.

(Mostly) Avoiding these blunders and making the odd good decision is how our conservative stock picking at the Dividend Freedom Tribe has led us to beating the Schwab US Dividend ETF (SCHD) by over 54% since inception in May 2020.

We'd be well served by taking a page out of Charlie Munger's book, who liked to quote Jacobi "Invert, always invert".

Instead of asking how to invest to do well, ask what would we do if we wanted to do badly.

Blunder 1: Blindly chasing high yields.

The high yield game is one that naturally attracts retail investors. High cash yields, in your account, are appealing.

But they fail to consider the first law of economics, which is that there is no free lunch.

Now of course there are exceptions to this. Time and again, we have bought dividend stocks when they yielded a lot due to market misconceptions and saw amazing capital gains.

This was certainly the case for IBM (IBM) which we acquired in between 2020 and 2022, only to see it rip in the past couple of years.

It was also the case of British American Tobacco (BTI), which after years of undervaluation and sky high yields, rerated higher.

I could go on and on.

Before you murder me in the comments, I absolutely believe it is possible to do high yield dividend investing with success.

I don't believe you can do so blindly. Because bar a few exceptions, when you get a high yield you're usually assuming one of the following risks:

  • Deterioration of revenues
  • Deterioration of profits

Both of which can cause the dividend to no longer be affordable by dividend investors.

Or sometimes you get investment vehicles which have a high yield on the sticker but are not actually giving you yield, they're just giving your capital back. This is the case for example of Eagle Eye Credit (ECC) a high income fund which claims that its objective is to:

generate high current income, with a secondary objective to generate capital appreciation.

Let me tell you. Management is full of it. They only have one objective: high current income. Capital appreciation isn't anywhere on the map.

This is an investment vehicle you want to stay away. A 29% yield is meaningless if its not coming from returns.

For instance in 2025, you started with a NAV of $8.38, by the end of the year your shares had a NAV of $5.7.

The company distributed $1.68 in dividends.

So they gave you back some of your cash, and destroyed $1 of it per share. But it isn't a viable way to do business.

Just look at the chart below, destruction of value in plain sight.

If you want to throw money out of the window, here's a better agreement:

  1. send me $10
  2. I'll send you $0.2 per month.
  3. I'll charge you $0.5 per year for the privilege.

Of course, I'm joking, but you get the point, don't chase yield unless you have a very clear narrative.

Blunder 2: Giving dividend history too much importance.

When you overindex on dividend history as a proof of safety, you can get blindsided by changing industry conditions, stock fundamentals, or both.

This causes you to believe in an illusion of safety. The prominence of dividend champions, dividend aristocrats, and other such lists which promote dividend longevity as a "be-all & end-all" signal amplify this.

Now let's be clear: all else held equal, you should trust a dividend with 20 years of track record more than one of 3 years.

The opportunity cost of cutting the former is much higher than the latter, so if there is a good path to maintaining the dividend, management might be inclined to keep it.

And also of course, the big problem comes from the fact that "all else" is never held "equal".

In 2025, there were a handful of high profile dividend cuts among high yielding stocks.

Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) stopped paying a dividend all together!

High income in 2023 turned into half the income in 2024, turned into no income in 2025, before it then went private.

What did you get instead? Destruction of your capital.

Remember return OF your capital should precede return ON your capital.

Dow Inc (DOW) cut its dividend in half in 2025.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), of which we were shareholders, also cut its dividend in what was very painful to our position in our portfolio.

(Yes we make mistakes and are suckers for a good management story at times too)

It takes a lifetime to build a reputation, and one dividend cut to break it, yet many management teams force themselves into a corner where they have no choice but to throw in the towel.

So when picking stocks, don't assume dividend longevity = safety.

Blunder 3: Holding on to your losers

I've seen the most retail money lost by "waiting to get even". This is anchoring to sunk costs.

Whatever the market is at now, that's where you're at. Forget if you're up 20 down 50. Ask: If I had to buy this now, would I? If I own it, would I hold this?

If the answer is no, then you need to cut it. Look, stock picking is not easy. If your bar for yourself is perfection, you'll end up on the wrong side of performance distribution.

When the writing is on the wall, you need to bail.

In June 2025, we finally accepted that our Conagra Brands (CAG) investment had been a bad one.

We should have realized this earlier. We quit the stock around $23. Today it is down to $17.

Wanting to "get even" would have cost us another 25%.

We reinvested those proceeds into our NextEra (NEE) position at $71 which increased 17% since then.

You cannot live in the past in investing.

Actually it's great advice for life. I often say that if people spent as much time thinking about the future as they do about the past, they'd have a much better life.

Blunder 4: Failing to diversify

In the investing world, whatever Warren Buffett has said is often taken as gospel. Buffett is known for having said:

Diversification is protection against ignorance. It makes little sense if you know what you are doing.

But this is bad advice for most of us. Markets today are not markets in the 60s when he actually applied this.

Peter Lynch said we should diversify because:

You should invest in several stocks because out of every five you pick one will be very great, one will be really bad, and three will be OK

And that's exactly how you should approach it.

You're gonna have a few really bad picks, a few amazing ones, and some ok ones.

Provided you cut the bad ones early enough, and let the winners run long enough, you'll still come out on top.

But 30,40,50 stocks is a good amount for diversification. You can concentrate somewhat in your top picks (we go to as much as 7% for our highest of high conviction picks), and otherwise have clear caps on a per position basis.

Some stuff won't ever go above 1% because its more speculative. Others get a 2-3% slot. Some 5-7%.

Blunder 5: Being too cheap for your own good.

There is a clear scale in doing things in conjunction with services. It ranges from:

Done for you, done with you, to do it yourself.

If you are a Seeking Alpha reader you are somewhere between a "done with you" and a "do it yourself" investor.

Investment funds would be the "done for you" variation. For some investors a set it and forget it index fund or ETF is exactly what they need. Minimum time spent on markets and move on.

I don't think that is most readers of Seeking Alpha, who are financially literate and want to build their own portfolio.

But you need to be realistic: How much can you do all by yourself? We cover a hundred stocks. That's 400 quarterly reports per year, on average one a day, but they all cluster, so it's much more intense than that.

Then we consider where these stocks fit in portfolios, if they should be watched or owned.

Then we consider sector allocation and where we want to be underweight/overweight given our views on where there is value and macro thesis.

If the answer is no, maybe you should outsource at least part of the research to those you trust and do your own due diligence. If that means giving up a few dollars in subscription fees, it might be worth it.

It doesn't need to be the Dividend Freedom Tribe. You could join any service, on SA or elsewhere that really works for you. But a few hundred dollars in subscription fees on portfolios of a few hundred thousand dollars truly pays for itself if you get a framework and research and portfolio construction guidance.

Conclusion

Before looking up, look down. I hope this is what this article got you to do. Avoid and sidestep these "rookie" mistakes, and investing will get an awful lot easier and more profitable for you.

And when you fumble, don't overthink it, even Steph Curry has missed 404 free throws over the course of his career.

You don't need to be perfect to be very good at this game, you just need to not be bad.

data.title: U.S. Vs. The World: Davos Outcomes May Redefine The Global Economy
data.description:
Background and Thesis

There’s a saying, often credited to Vladimir Lenin, that “There are decades where nothing happens, and there are weeks where decades happen.” Now, with President Donald Trump at Davos, we could quickly find the world racing through minutes in which years and years unfold. Up until now, many global leaders had been going out of their way to get and stay on President Trump’s good side. Yet years of diplomacy, deals, and laboring throughout both of Trump’s terms may quickly unravel as both friends and rivals gear up to clash with the United States. The impact of these developments on stock indices could be profound, and while the short-term turbulence may be the focus for many investors, the long-term implications could be even more crucial not just for portfolios, but for economies the world over.

American stock markets surged out of the gate this morning after President Trump seemingly ruled out force to take Greenland, which may soothe tensions a bit. Yet even if the Administration sticks to a less forceful posture, we may already be starting down a slippery slope toward economic multipolarity, great power competition, and a (likely limited) rollback of globalization. While many world leaders, and particularly NATO members, had previously worked to appease President Trump, we’re now seeing clear signs that much of the world is starting to dig in.

Canada has extended an oil branch to China. Much of South America and the European Union will soon be tied together, forming the largest free trade bloc on Earth. Greenland looks like a red line for Denmark and most of greater Europe. President Trump once again argued for the United States taking control of Greenland at Davos, but crucially, he seems to be ruling out force, stating “I don't have to use force, I don't want to use force, I won't use force.” The Trump Administration seems set to continue to diplomatically pursue Greenland, but I find it highly, highly unlikely that Denmark and Greenland will ever cave, setting the stage for future market turmoil.

When investing, it can be tempting to ignore all the messy politics. During stable, prosperous times, ignoring a lot of the politics could be fine. Yet ultimately, markets and politics are deeply intertwined and major political developments could have a huge impact on the private sector. After the Trump Administration reached a variety of trade deals, some signed, some not, I was beginning to assume that political challenges would cool a bit, even if still simmering. Yet tensions have flared up in recent weeks, with Greenland becoming a lightning rod. The risk of a rapture, especially in the rather integrated “West,” could have dramatic impacts on the market.

Following President Trump’s Davos speech as well as the comments made by other leaders at Davos, tensions seem destined to remain strained, and I believe we’re entering a world of multipolarity, with countries and blocs the world over likely to turn inward, emphasizing domestic economies, diversifying trade and supply chains, and perhaps stepping back a bit regarding globalization. The global economy has become highly integrated over the last several decades, but now there’s a major risk of backsliding.

Regarding the impact of political developments, the long-term changes are arguably the most important for investors. That said, political turmoil can also have a major impact in the near term. Stock markets across Europe have declined substantially over the past few days. Gold and silver have been floating upward. Bitcoin has seen big swings both ways. I believe geographic diversification is increasingly important for investors. Efforts among middle powers to increase self-sufficiency and rely less on great powers, like the United States, could propel their respective economies and stock markets.

Meanwhile, the dollar has been on a slide throughout much of President Trump’s second term, which will obviously have a big impact on many American investors. Going forward, we’re likely to see increased efforts to diversify away from the dollar. While full de-dollarization isn’t likely to occur anytime soon, even moderate diversification could further fuel the dollar’s decline. At the very least, investors should explore diversifying away from the dollar or otherwise mitigating the potential impacts of a sliding currency.

Dueling Speeches: Canadian PM Carney Searches For A Third Way, President Trump Marches Onward

President Trump has once again emphasized the “need” for Greenland to be controlled by the United States. The core of his argument is that Denmark, home to only about six million people, simply doesn’t have the capacity to defend Greenland (primarily from China and/or Russia). Yet crucially, President Trump seems to be ruling out force to take Greenland. This fueled a sharp bump in markets.

Still, ominously, the President has said that the United States “will remember” if Europe doesn’t hand over Greenland. What this means isn’t clear so far. The biggest question, in my mind, is whether force is permanently off the table, or if this is simply a temporary breather to focus on diplomatic efforts. In the latter case, I don’t believe Denmark will ever surrender Greenland, meaning a future geopolitical rupture may be unavoidable.
